text: And a lot of this is very non-transparent now because most of these ships that are in that region have basically turned their transponders off. Some vessels have gone through. I don't know if those vessels were tanker vessels or, you know, all tanker vessels, but we do know some vessels have gone through. The problem is really Iran's ability now to hit these tankers. And I think the last count I saw was 16 plus tankers had been hit or damaged in some way. So, they are, they obviously do not want to be targeted by Iran. And so, what used to be a way where we could track a lot of these ships is now essentially gone with all these transponders shut off. So, I think the, what's happened now is we, there are hundreds of tankers stuck in the, in the Gulf of Amman. And a lot of the producers, all producers in the region now have nowhere, they're running out of storage. If they haven't already run out of storage because it used to be, you'd pump the oil, you'd stick it in storage, you'd wait for a vessel to come in, you'd load it and you'd ship it. Well, now the vessels are stuck and the storage is full. So, we are actually seeing oil production closed because they have nowhere to store the oil. So, I think what the market is coming to grips with is the fact that even if we were to get the, the straight open, there's going to be a production gap because it's just, it's been shut in. Now, the IEA, along with the, you know, everyone in that group, and I think there's 30 something countries that are involved in this, did make the decision to release 400 million barrels from emergency oil supplies globally. And that, it's hard to say that that didn't do any good because, you know, who knows where we would have gone had that not happened. But it didn't seem to have a huge impact on the market in the United States. You know, our portion of that release from the strategic petroleum reserve is around 172 million barrels. We currently, at least as of last week, had about 415 million barrels. So, that is a significant portion of our current strategic reserve.

chunk_id: Energy update on a perfectly timed Friday the 13th_0005
text: We currently, at least as of last week, had about 415 million barrels. So, that is a significant portion of our current strategic reserve. But it didn't seem to have a huge impact on the market in the United States. You know, our portion of that release from the strategic petroleum reserve is around 172 million barrels. And that, it's hard to say that that didn't do any good because, you know, who knows where we would have gone had that not happened. Again, I think there's no lack of oil here in the United States. I mean, we're pumping record amounts of oil. You know, we're not dependent on that, but certainly all over Europe is. So, the, it's really Brent crude that is the biggest problem, although WTI is following Brent quite closely. So, when you look at, you know, what are some of the bigger analysts talking about when, or some of the bigger analysts doing, they're all raising their projections for average price of crude oil, mainly because of just the unknown component. We just don't know if the straight is going to be closed for weeks or potentially months. And from what I've, what I've read from many analysts here recently is if we're talking about a situation where crude oil is shut in and we can't get it through the straight for a course of, you know, a month or two, that would move, that would go beyond the 400 million barrels release. And we could be talking 120 to 150 dollar Brent crude oil quite easily. So, the risk is certainly high. We'll have to see literally how things play out on a day by day basis, whether the military can get in there and do something to stop these, these drone attacks and get vessels passing through, through the straight. And there's been a lot of other little small levers that have been pulled here recently, something with regard to the Jones Act that allows us to, at least in the United States, transport, transport oil via foreign vessels from one port to another, there was a rule that couldn't happen.
